NB that shunit2 is a shell script that we download and that gets activated by saying `source ./shunit2`. We inject that ourselves to ensure the relpath is correct.

We don't technically need to do this validation that the user hasn't specified it - we could allow the user to have two `source shunit2` lines. But that almost never seems to be desirable - in the best case, we run the tests twice; in the more likely case, the relpath they use is different than what we need to inject. This is meant to be a better UX.

The regex here has to be flexible to handle cases like `source ${HERE}/shunit2`, which the tests cover.

```
try:
```

then if the import did run, you can do:


@gjoliver,

I think the question is what do you want to happen if a user sets max_seq_len > sgd_minibatch_size

The current code throws an exception but it only does so during training and only after the policy learns to generate episodes that last longer than sgd_minibatch_size. In StatelessCartpole it has to train for ~50 iterations before it asserts. That is a long time to wait to find out your config is set up incorrectly. The current assertion does not tell you that the config is the issue so you have to decode that.

One way to fix this is to slice based on min of (max_seq_len, sgd_minibatch_size) but if you do this you are ignoring the users request that truncated-bptt should be at most max_seq_len. So in the example in the issue you would be changing 200 -> =128. 


A second way to fix this is to test the configuration and warn the user ahead of time. The question is where to test. 

a. validate_config: Easy to test the config but no easy way to see if the model uses state and max_seq_len should even be considered. Currently some tests are failing because they fail the check but it should not matter because they do not use states.

b. At policy construction time. Then we could check policy.is_recurrent() and only if it is True do we do this config check.

c. Put it in do_minibatch_sgd. This might be the real place we want to put it since it is not actually about PPO but the sgd process.

I have been trying to get rid of this manipulation coz I do think it's not a good practice here. But the intention behind this is primarily trying to be able to search for a list of `(key, uuid)` pair that can match with the input command + dependencies in the database. If we take a look in the schema we have, `cl_bundle_dependency` has the following columns:
|index| column | 
|-- |-- |
| 1. | child_uuid | 
|2. | child_path |
|3.| parent_uuid|
|4.| parent_path|

`child_uuid` is the target bundle that we will be searching for. `key` will map to `child_path` and `uuid` will map to `parent_uuid`. Given that the relationship between `child_uuid` and `child_path (key), parent_uuid (uuid)` is `1-to-n` mapping, when we group by `child_uuid`, we need some ways to keep the records in the other two columns together. I have been searching for solutions to keep those records without doing any concatenation, but so far I haven't found anything yet. If you guys @teetone or @percyliang have any suggestions, please let me know.

@rabernat @halehawk, `ensure_ndarray` takes a view onto anything that is a new-style buffer and returns it as a NumPy array (also a new-style buffer). Though it is just the same compressed 1-D `uint8` array of data. Mainly it acts as a type checker as it raises on anything that is not a new-style buffer. It also gives us something easier to manipulate. That said, we could cast it to a `memoryview` after if you prefer. The main point is `ensure_ndarray` is basically free whereas `ensure_bytes` always copies (unless it is actually a `bytes` object). Would be better not to copy if we can avoid it both for performance and memory usage reasons.

Here is a description of the header:

> Optional and valid only for the setAccessControl operation. Sets POSIX access control rights on files and directories. The value is a comma-separated list of access control entries that fully replaces the existing access control list (ACL). Each access control entry (ACE) consists of a scope, a type, a user or group identifier, and permissions in the format "[scope:][type]:[id]:[permissions]". The scope must be "default" to indicate the ACE belongs to the default ACL for a directory; otherwise scope is implicit and the ACE belongs to the access ACL. There are four ACE types: "user" grants rights to the owner or a named user, "group" grants rights to the owning group or a named group, "mask" restricts rights granted to named users and the members of groups, and "other" grants rights to all users not found in any of the other entries. The user or group identifier is omitted for entries of type "mask" and "other". The user or group identifier is also omitted for the owner and owning group. The permission field is a 3-character sequence where the first character is 'r' to grant read access, the second character is 'w' to grant write access, and the third character is 'x' to grant execute permission. If access is not granted, the '-' character is used to denote that the permission is denied. For example, the following ACL grants read, write, and execute rights to the file owner and john.doe@contoso, the read right to the owning group, and nothing to everyone else: "user::rwx,user:john.doe@contoso:rwx,group::r--,other::---,mask=rwx". > However, the file handle issue you mention is already present: any back pointer traversed opens a file descriptor. Making get work on an immutable pointer would only really add one more file descriptor open.

Understood.

The resource exhaustion attack I had in mind becomes relevant once we hook the RPC and P2P network interfaces up to the Clarity VM database to allow clients to query key/value pairs.  Because these calls can be concurrent, the MARF's `get` will either need to take an immtuable self reference (possibly by way of creating the initial file descriptor on the stack), or we'll want to create a bunch of read-only thread-local Clarity VM database handles (each of which contains a read-only MARF struct) and give each P2P and PRC thread its own MARF.  I'm in favor of the latter approach because it lets us bound the number of file descriptors used by the MARF globally in a very natural way -- it's a function of the (fixed) number of threads we allocate to these other subsystems.  We can also continue to allow `get` to take a mutable self reference.  Trying to accomplish something similar by tracking the number of concurrent `get` calls on a single thread-shared MARF sounds much hairier by comparison.  But we don't have to solve this problem today.

In case anyone else wonders "why don't we fix the iterator class", here's why:

- "Iterator" is [this thing](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/pytypes.h#L836)
- [`operator*`](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/pytypes.h#L857-L863) returns a [`py::handle`](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/master/include/pybind11/pytypes.h#L841) which is generally better to copy than to take by reference.
- The iterator stores and mutates `value`, so returning a reference here would be wrong as soon as you advance the iterator.

```
def some_handler(external_arg_needed, callback_arg):

    callback("callback_arg")

def make_callback():
    cb = partial(some_handler, "first arg value")

```

Then the constraint that ``tr(X) == 1`` becomes ``a + b == 1`` and the objective ``tr_inv(X)`` is equal to ``1 / a + 1 / (1 - a)``, which leads to an optimal value achieved at ``a = b = 1/2``. ok, so the polygon for the full disk looks like this:
![full_disk](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/167802/216694816-ff2b1207-f684-4b9b-9d43-8f1eed8e1a9c.png)

Now, if we just clip y at 2e6 like it is in main, we get this:
![clipped_y](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/167802/216694914-1ff73d65-0a63-49e0-a3f2-518135dbf858.png)

If you look carefully, you see the last point on each side of the y=2e6 line ends up in space, because we 'project' the extreme points of the equator onto a latitude where the sphere is not as wide.

So how do we avoid this? 
One solution would be to not compute everything below y=2e6 and find the edge of the disk at that y position to close the polygon. It's not too hard, but there are many different case, whether the disk is cut horizontally, vertically, or both, includes the equator or prime meridian, or not.
A simpler geometrical approach in my opinion is just to find the intersection of the full disk polygon with the rectangle of the area extent. Since they are all in the same projection, we can see that as a simple 2d geometry problem.
![full_and_box](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/167802/216699413-0833cade-4032-439a-9531-7a3ffc53e9df.png)

`python setup.py develop` or `pip install -ve .` does two things is the setuptools world:

1. run `python setup.py dist_info` so that the right meta-data is generated
2. adds a `.pth` file where ever you are installing to add the path to where the source is to the PYTHONPATH at start up

For security reasons (I think), `.pth` files are only used when they are in the "standard" search path not in a folder added by `PYTHONPATH`, those paths are all read only so the overlay trick does not work here.  However, you can still do these two things manually or run `pip install ....` everytime you make a change (which if you wrap it up in a helper script that then restarts bsui might not be that bad).

In this case it was not absolutely necessary, because we can rely on the fact that django itself is tested for this, but a few lines of extra tests are better than lack of tests, so we can keep it, but we need to make sure this test is robust:
you're checking only that a `ValidationError` is raised, but it may be raised for different reasons. For example, a contributor may change the code in a way that another `ValidationError` is raised by this function, while the error you expect is not raised, in that case this test would pass anyway, that's why robust tests are more specific in the expected outcome.

In these cases we always have to check the contents of the `ValidationError`, like its done in other openwisp modules:, see here: https://github.com/openwisp/openwisp-controller/pull/266/files#diff-3fef99a26498e5609812504f227b4004R224-R230

@kaushikb11 that's a really good idea and also very useful—I'm not sure about the best way to implement this, but this is what I'm thinking for now:
1. create a function decorator that inspects the source and locates all Exceptions/Errors that are raised
2. creates a flow control graph of the function to be executed
3. quickly trace this graph with the given inputs and determine if any errors would be raised (sort of like TensorFlow 2's autograph/JIT tracing)
    - if no errors are raised, yay! proceed
    - if errors are raised, determine which variable triggered the exception and determine allowed values and update the kwarg to an allowed value
    - I think we _need_ steps 1 and 2 because if we trace without simplifying the code graph, we'll end up actually executing code during the trace (which would mean code is executed twice, which we don't want)

By default helper.make_model will produce a model with the latest IR_VERSION, which means giving an older opset_version make_model will create a model with older opset_version but with the latest ir_version. However, it even brings more updates than before. The reason is typically when a contributor introduced a new node test data, at that moment the opset_version has been already bumped, but the ir_version has not been bumped before the release. Thus most cases those node test data were using opset_version with (corresponding ir_version -1). This PR will make them use corresponding ir_version instead, which makes more sense to me.

Thanks for the additional info. I agree that the current docs are far from good and are noisy because they provide lots of information about the models in an unstructured way. For example:
1. They explain how to use the models during inference.
2. They talk about the training data and the normalization values.
3. They provide random info on where the pre-trained models are stored, the `train()/eval()` calls, the accuracies, etc

Some of this information might be obsolete (for example all the inference info should be re-written with the presets in mind) but some is still valid and just needs to be transferred to the right place (for example the category mapping between Pascal and COCO for segmentation, info related to where the pretrained weights are stored etc). My concern for removing the latter prematurely is that for a period the live docs will be missing critical information. Correct me if I'm wrong but I understand that after merging this, we won't even have a list of the available models linking from the documentation. This is why I said earlier that it could be hard for the community to restore some of this info (they would have to dig on the previous versions of the documentation and recover the useful parts which got deleted).

One approach to mitigate the above is to only remove the info you replace. We can follow the same approach in every PR sent by the community (aka that the transfer of info on the new format is correct and that things were cleaned up correctly). We can also ensure that a deep cleaning is done at the end by reviewing the documentation as a whole (similar to what we did with the final clean up steps at #4652).

As mentioned above, another approach could be to keep a feature branch (which will certainly allow you to start from a clean slate) but the problem with that is you don't want to leave this open for too long to avoid conflicts. Also the community won't be credited for their PRs because in the end only 1 PR will land on main. 

Let me know your thoughts. Happy to have an offline chat if you prefer to speed this up.

The `ddtrace` logger's parent logger is `root`. I can't think of a situation where this needs to be `True` since this may lead to some unexpected logging behavior when interacting with custom loggers in an application. 

I did some digging around past issues and only found recommendations to disable the propagation, like this: https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/issues/3306 and https://github.com/DataDog/dd-trace-py/issues/3288.

I'm open to adding an environment variable for `True` if there's a good use case for it though!

I want to have tested `object -> json` conversion, mostly, and the checksum testing then just verifies that `json -> hex digest` works correctly.

To remove the import of the private method, I'm thinking I can make a public object representing the keyword arguments we pass to `json.dumps()` in `hash_utils.py`, and use that in our `stable_json_hash()` method and in our tests here. But to me, it seems extremely reasonable to test the *stable* json conversion (a property we rely upon!) in isolation, while explicitly marking it as an implementation detail and not to be used by itself. To me, making an explicit entrypoint to stable json conversion is reasonable even though it results in the import of a private method.

Here's the gist of it: for the Python API we chain the exceptions. The user will get an OrderError with a brief, unambiguous message and this carries the BadQuery exception with it. And then we pass the responsibility for presenting the BadQuery message to a human onto the caller of create_order. Advantage for the Python API is that we generalize our exception so that it can suit the needs of almost any caller and eliminate code. Advantage for a user is that they are not bound by choices we make.

Thinking out loud on this: my pre-existing viewpoint has definitely that we should be agnostic to semantics of the constraints, instead of offering particular constraints like "min_length". That said, the semantics of these constraints are fairly unambiguous. I'm still leery though. Here are some of the considerations:
* If we have special-casing of particular constraints, I could see a slippery slope where users beg us to add additional ones or submit PRs to add additional ones.  Even if we decide to tether ourselves to frictionless, the frictionless spec will likely expand over time, and then there will be the question of which version of frictionless each version of Dagster supports.
* Someone who wants to translate their own constraint library to Dagster constraints needs to look at each constraint and understand whether it matches a Dagster constraint, rather than just pass through their string representation.
* People might have specific ways that they want to render common constraints. E.g. someone might want to render a constraint as "minimum: 5", while someone else might want to render the same constraint as ">= 5".

@schrockn might have thoughts.

This isn't a big concern or anything, more of a "is this a code smell?" type question.

By passing pointers around and using their addresses to check if we have cached the decrypted value or not, we are heavily relying on something very implicit here.

If the implementation of `NewSecretProperty` were to wrap the `resource.Secret` or `resource.PropertyValue` in some other type then it's possible our caching solution would stop working. That's probably unlikely and maybe not even possible.

But what I'm getting at is that rather than perhaps adding a comment "IMPORTANT! Beside to not mess with these pointers, because things won't get cached if you copy the values and change their addresses." it might be better to instead formalize what we need. Specifically, that every secret is given some stable identifiers.

Currently that is the address of `resource.Secret`, but you can imagine it being `resource.Secret::ID` or something else. That's perhaps a bigger, more tedious refactoring than what we need or want. But It seems like that might not be a bad idea, since then it's clear what we are depending on.

Unfortunately, how the string extraction works doesn't use the same AST parser as I used in the i18nSyncTransfer work (where I already took the glob approach). I thought it was a great idea before I started working - but as I got into it I quickly realized that it would be biting off more than is necessary to chew.

The AST parsers have minute details about them that are different - they call a node differently or structure the node metadata differently or they require that you use whatever weird specific options you need to accommodate features you're using or not using - so it isn't as easy as just using the existing ExtractStrings.js code and replacing webpack's build system w/ a different one.

Which is why I figured that adding some kind of condition to when/how that plugin is actually run would basically have the same ultimate outcome: working code with a single command (package.json script anyway) that extracts/profiles the messages.

From the sound of things, it feels like the test updates the fuzzy modal's search field and triggers the filterJobs function by simulating an `enter` keypress, but then doesn't wait for filterJobs to actually filter the list of jobs before testing that it worked. filterJobs updates the lists stored in state and that state gets updated as the list of options in the UI, but that UI update can take some time to be reflected in the DOM, so the tests might be looking at a pre-update version of the list. 

I had suggested on Matrix that it might be sufficient to just wait X seconds (2 to 5 would probably be enough) between triggering filterJobs and expecting the list of jobs to be filtered.

Another less dirty option might be modifying filterJobs to emit some custom "job list filtered" event whenever it's sure to have a fully updated DOM version of the list, and then the test code could wait to observe that custom event before performing the actual tests.

The behaviors tested are slightly different. Here are the three relevant methods at a high level:

1. `Scope.parse` does a full parse and provides a list of `Scope`s as the result.
2. `Scope.deserialize` runs `Scope.parse` and then asserts that the length of the resulting list is exactly 1 -- and it unwraps that value.
3. `Scope.__init__` does not do any parsing. It takes any input string and assigns it to the `Scope.scope_string` attribute, after checking that it was a valid value to use

`__init__` does no parsing and rejects these strings because this is a simpler design than some alternatives for breaking the potential for a `parse -> __init__ -> parse` cycle. (I considered the path of a true alternate constructor which does not call `__init__`, but I thought that wasn't worth it in the short term.)

So this test ensures that `Scope("foo bar")` is rejected "because you can't initialize a scope whose string contains a space anywhere".

By contrast, the above test is checking that "if you deserialize a scope string, it must parse such that there is only one top-level or 'root' scope". Spaces are allowed in cases like `Scope.deserialize("foo[bar baz]")`.

Right. We are trying to encompass multiple pieces of information in a single blob key. As you mentioned, this is a result of the design that conforms to the DataProvider abstraction, in which there is not a natural fit for efficient look up of 
 individual ops in graphs. So we encode information in blob keys, as an escape hatch. 

This approach is currently okay, because `run_name` is hard-coded to `__default_debugger_run__`. Each logdir currently is mandated to have at most only one run of tfdbg2 data. So there is no ambiguity when run name is included as the tail of the blob key, preceded by a `.`. When we support multiple debugger runs in the same logdir in the future, we can continue enforcing a run name pattern that doesn't contain `.`s.

We can see whether there are any cancel scopes active, but that includes inherited scopes, that don't correspond to a `with` block inside the exiting task. And, if someone does detach inside a `with <cancel scope>` block, then it's true that they'll eventually get some kind of explosion in the `__exit__` method, but I don't think this will corrupt Trio's internal state; it'll just be a regular exception.

For nurseries OTOH, it's easy to check for, and killing a task with active nurseries inside would break Trio's internal invariants.

> on the contrary, it does pass unicode actually

I meant the `isoformat()` from the standard library. As the shared snippet showed,
it only accepts `str` values, and raises on `unicode` separators. Here is another snippet with more details (checked out from [your branch](https://github.com/meysam81/python-jalali/)), running in a virtualenv with Python 2:

```
(_venv) farzad@farzad-thinkpad ~/t/python-jalali (isoformat/timespec)> python
Python 2.7.15 (default, Oct 15 2018, 15:26:09) 
[GCC 8.2.1 20180801 (Red Hat 8.2.1-2)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> sep_str = 'T'
>>> type(sep_str)
<type 'str'>
>>> datetime.now().isoformat(sep_str)  # str is accepted
'2019-07-08T21:48:21.059422'
>>> sep_unicode = u'T'
>>> type(sep_unicode)
<type 'unicode'>
>>> datetime.now().isoformat(sep_unicode)  # unicode is not
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: isoformat() argument 1 must be char, not unicode
>>> 
```
The exception message explicitly says `unicode` is not accepted.

Now, the behavior in the test accepting `unicode` but failing on `str` is actually the opposite of the standard library (desired behavior). We're experiencing this behavior because `jdatetime` [uses UTF-8](https://github.com/slashmili/python-jalali/blob/4e429273e660c8cb3d749bbda229032cd961909c/jdatetime/__init__.py#L1) as source file encoding, so all string literals in the source file are `unicode` not `str`. So the [default value](https://github.com/meysam81/python-jalali/blob/d03f3b920eebb17068f16b859cdda015d42fdde8/jdatetime/__init__.py#L1215) for `sep` parameter (literal "T"), is a `unicode`not a `str`, as clearly demonstrated here:

```
(_venv) farzad@farzad-thinkpad ~/t/python-jalali (isoformat/timespec)> python
Python 2.7.15 (default, Oct 15 2018, 15:26:09) 
[GCC 8.2.1 20180801 (Red Hat 8.2.1-2)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import jdatetime
>>> jdatetime.datetime.isoformat.func_defaults
(u'T', u'auto')
>>> 
```

With a `to_` instance method, both the "source" and the "destination" types are pinned down at definition time (source type by the `self` type, destination by the return type). This means the caller at the call site has no freedom to pick the destination type other than by looking for an appropriate `to_` method. If such a method doesn't exist, the caller is just out of luck.

With a `from_` classmethod, the call site controls the target type, because the call site does `Foo.from_x()` and `Foo` becomes the `cls` argument. This not only gives us more flexibility and convenience, but also means that we get more functionality out of fewer from-to function pairs.

So let me see if I am following what you are saying correctly. We may locally expect that a partner has pending locks in his state and [generate](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/1466689bd21bf7a4e285784bc502b3620fdc44ba/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L1792) a `ContractSendChannelBatchUnlock` during `handle_channel_settled`.

Let's assume we closed the channel, and he may for whatever reason not have called `updateTransfer()`.

In that case then the onchain locksroot would be an `EMPTY_HASH` and the linked logic would break. Is this what you are speaking about?

In the case where we are sending an onchain unlock for all pending locks from `partner` to `participant` the onchain locksroot **must be** the same as the offchain one. This is enforced by the [contract](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden-contracts/blob/ea259567c8d7b06410e7969d5a653953eaf39be9/raiden_contracts/contracts/TokenNetwork.sol#L814-L823). So I would say in that case the state machine should never have generated the `ContractSendChannelBatchUnlock`. This does not seem to be the case at the moment:

https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/1466689bd21bf7a4e285784bc502b3620fdc44ba/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L1788-1807

So perhaps we should add a check inside `handle_channel_settled` to see if the onchain locksroot matches what we expect to send and if not, just don't generate the `ContractSendChannelBatchUnlock`?

I agree each model has its pros and cons:
- The dictionary model is good if we have a bunch of optional params. 
   - The downside is we need to define an `id` for each param and then include it in the message (so if we have a lot of optional parameters and most often use many of them, the dictionary model can add the overhead of including the id bytes).
   - We use this model  for dataset (where we have a lot of optional param and a subset can be provided).
- The empty struct `t()` model has the advantage that the spinel format can mirror the ot API call format (when we have pointers to params that can be `null` if not provided). It is more useful when the params are often used. 
  - We use this in few  cases (not that many), e.g. `STREAM` props (for example for PHY data or vendor data), or `ADDRESS_CACHE_TABLE`  or somewhat similar in `MESHCOP_COMMISSIONER_JOINERS` (empty struct to indicate any joiner).

I sat down at 1am thinking this out, and I'm pretty sure the following is equivalent to both what upstream is doing and what this is doing:

```python
error %= self.inputRange
if error > self.inputRange / 2:
    return error - self.inputRange
```

2. Allow None as max_results but internally we query the feed details to get the total number of data points on the feed.

Class doesn't make this very easy either. Overriding can absolutely be achieved by making a child class, overriding the `simplify` method by essentially the same code with a different function, or by monkey patching that function. Indeed this isn't ideal, but it's not impossible.

However I don't see any reason why the user would ever need to override the simplify implementation. If there's something that our logic misses, it should be reported as a bug, not solved locally by overrides.

This could however be simplified if absolutely necessary, by moving the logic of our `simplify` function into `simplify.py` too, and calling it, with the `get_unused_elements` as an argument to it. That way, overriding would be much simpler, by just importing `get_unused_elements`, using that in some other function, which unions it's result with some other custom obtained element IDs. With that, all that would be needed is to make a child Motd class, override the `simplify` method to call the `simplify` function that's now in `simplifies.py`, and pass in a custom function instead of just the `get_unused_elements`.

Then again, I still don't really think that this needs to be "easily overridable", there's no reason anyone should need to override the `simplify` method.

Do we want these to be absolute or relative?

Relative has the advantage that it allows a single frontend to be exposed across multiple domain names and clients would generate the appropriate URLs.  Of course, we could I suppose extract this from the incoming `/api/uploader` request and do the substitution server-side; I could see an argument for either approach.

Another possibility would be adding a template placeholder for the base URL, which if included is basically the same as a relative URL, but could be omitted in the actual template to allow the frontend to return an absolute URL if it wanted to avoid ambiguity (e.g. if wanted to do that on prod).

We are now adding copyright and license information in a special header to the files. You can follow the following pattern.
```
# <one liner describing file content>
# Copyright (C) 2019  <names go here>
#
# Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy
# of this software and associated documentation files (the "Software"), to deal
# in the Software without restriction, including without limitation the rights
# to use, copy, modify, merge, publish, distribute, sublicense, and/or sell
# copies of the Software, and to permit persons to whom the Software is
# furnished to do so, subject to the following conditions:
#
# The above copyright notice and this permission notice shall be included in all
# copies or substantial portions of the Software.
#
# THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
# IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
# FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
# AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
# LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
# OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
# SOFTWARE.
#
# You should have received a copy of the MIT License along with this program. If
# not, see <https://opensource.org/licenses/MIT>.
r"""
The rest of the docstring goes here.

Examples:

more stuff...

etc
"""
```
The reason is that as an open source project, we should have the license information at the head of every file (slowly working to get there), but we don't want to have this information on every page of the documentation. The comment using `#` is ignored by the documentation so that only the part in triple quotes `"""` is used for docstrings.

In fact this can only be used by the user - it's not used internally. Here's how you'd use it: If you have a box in which a dislocation is meant to be inserted, you might have an orientation like [[1, 1, 1], [-1, 0, 1], [1, -2, 1]]. In which case, you can either create a box from the beginning with this orientation, or you rotate the elastic tensor. Since creating a box with a weird orientation both increases the number of atoms (and hence the computation time) and decreases the number of symmetries phonopy finds, usually it makes more sense to rotate the elastic tensor. And those who are familiar with the anisotropic elasticity theory (I can't remember who did the work) might know that a full elastic tensor is required to parametrise the strain field around a dislocation for the given orientation. So this function totally makes sense for such a case.

* Consider non-greedy `...(.+?)...` to avoid matching multiple `<script..>...</script>` within the capture group.
* Isn't there a terminator like `;` that should be mentioned, so eg `...(.+?;)\s<script>'`?
* Instead of the spaces, `\s[^>]*?` is robust against changes of whitespace and unexpected new attributes.
* You could use the formulation `(?P<q1>"|\'|\b)attribute_name(?P=q1) to be robust against changes in or omission of quoting, but then you'd also have to use `...(?P<schema_obj>.+?)...` and add `group='schema_obj'` to the arguments.
* There could also be whitespace in the attribute expressions, eg `...data-react-helmet\s*=\s*"true"...`.
* Ideally the pattern would be robust against changes in attribute order, but there's no way to specify a match of two patterns A, B regardless of order, except to write `(?P<A>A)B(?(A)|A)`.

I largely agree with you. Running everything (tests + release logic) on a single tag, and not take care of the actual branch or commits on it will simplify the workflow. In the very improbable situation where the first tag would fail to build the release artifacts (or fail on the integration tests), we can just push a new version of it (like `v0.40.0-1`). By resolving the actual version of Certbot independently from the tag name, the new pipeline will retry to push the `0.40.0` version of Certbot.

On that matter, I think it is preferable to always trigger the pipeline through a tag, and not from the Azure DevOps UI. I think there is some valuable insight to keep track through tags in the GIT repo of failed releases pipelines.

Then I think it is clear that the `advanced`/`release` pipeline should be triggered: 
* on push to `test-*` branches
* on push to `v*` tags
* triggered nightly

Now, about the existence of a dedicated `release` pipeline for push to `v*` tags. To go toward your approach, it is true that in a DevOps approach, it is desirable that the integration branch (here `master`) is always deployable. This means that we should be able to extract from it at anytime a proper and stable version of Certbot. On that extend you do not even need anymore dedicated release branches, and just tag from `master`. This is the approach used in the [GitHub flow](https://gitversion.readthedocs.io/en/latest/git-branching-strategies/githubflow/)

This is an open door to Continuous Deployment, since you can trigger a release anytime it is required, even after each PR merged if you want.

In that perspective, there is no dedicated `release` pipeline. There is only the `advanced` one, that constitutes your oracle to say that `master` branch is deployable.

I have only two concerns about having only the `advanced` pipeline:
* we are not on a CD approach _yet_: it means that a lot of human intervention is required to build a release, and I am a little concern that some wrong artifacts could be mistakenly taken from the `advanced` pipeline
* if we go someday to the so-called Azure `Releases Pipelines`, it may be more cumbersome to define a proper trigger if all CI pipelines are merged for the various situations of on-demand tests, nightly tests and release tags builds.

If these two concerns seems reasonable to you, I will go to the merged of the two pipelines. Take into account that having two pipelines is still very lightweight to maintain thanks to the job templates, and most of the work will be done in them, not in the pipelines themselves.

I would like also, if we drop the scripts about the GitHub release changelog, that it is done manually during a Certbot release because I think there is a lot of values to get the dedicated changelog on the associated GitHub release.

Two optimizations I see:
1. The `AscendantAddresses` object for each `import * source_root` will be effectively unique, so relatively little reuse can happen between these. It would probably be more efficient to list all targets below each source root with `DescendantAddresses`, (because it would not be module specific). Those could then be re-used to match owned sources.
2. The expansion of `AddressSpecs` is batched (the motivation was to be able to handle a case like "`--spec-file` containing a line per target in the repo"), so the effect of putting all of the `AscendantAddresses` for the imports of one target in one`AddressSpecs` object is that none of the per-import expansion of `AscendantAddresses` is reused _across_ targets. Instead, if each import was a separate `AddressSpecs` object, then would be memoized.

This is to deal with nested object IDs. Before, if object A contained object B, then the owner of A would always find out about A before B. But with the new protocol, the borrower of B can bypass the reference tree, so the owner can find out about B first. So we shouldn't consider B's ref count to be 0 until the owner also finds out about A.

To be honest, I'm not completely sure why this didn't come up before. If you think it's too complicated, we could scrap the changes to the ref counting protocol and just have the borrower's raylet periodically check if the owner still has the object in scope instead.

All of this is good background, but I don't think it necessarily addresses Tommy's core concern that users accustomed to periodic boundaries may be confused by this. The core problem is that freud's convention for what is "inside" is documented in the tutorials I wrote [here](https://freud.readthedocs.io/en/latest/gettingstarted/examples/module_intros/box.Box.html?highlight=origin#Using-boxes) and [here](https://freud.readthedocs.io/en/latest/gettingstarted/tutorial/periodic.html?highlight=origin#periodic-boundary-conditions), but not in the Box class itself. The latter link is referenced in the Box docstring, but you have to follow the link and it's still kind of hidden in a note at the bottom.

I suggest we move that note to the docstring for the Box class so that we clearly indicate the convention that boxes go from -L/2 to L/2, then reference that here (something like "where inside is defined by the conventions defined by :class:`~.Box`, ignoring periodicity"). Then we have something unambiguous that we can point to when users are confused as to why (0.9, 0, 0) is not inside a box of size (1, 1, 1).
I think it's worthwhile to retain this high-level overview of what memoization / versioning is at the top.

I am not sure this can work. 

- The main change in https://github.com/openthread/openthread/pull/6849 is that it adds a new behavior and new flag `mIsHeaderUpdated ` in the interaction between OT core (mac/sub-mac) and the radio platform 
  - This flag may be set/cleared by either side.
- This in turn required changes to the spinel frame formats between host and RCP to add support for the new flag. 
- The changes you have in this PR make the spinel frame to be parsed correctly based on the RCP API version, but we still need to convey the flag between radio platform and OT stack.
  - I don't think there is any easy default value or behavior we can use for this flag (when RCP version is old and does not support it).

- using `@cached_property` is more explicit than caching "under the hood", so it is a hint to the programmer to use it carefully
- other important properties on IRnode (like `valency`, `contains_self_call`) are already implicitly or explicitly cached properties. so we already need to be careful with this, and in fact we already have tests which trip when we mess up caching (i.e. not deconstructing and reconstructing IRnodes properly).

> It is pretty hard to reason about the object store size, especially since it's unclear to a user exactly how large the preprocessed dataset will be.
> 
> Ultimately, we expect the dataset optimizer to automatically determine the best configuration for this right? If not, then we may want to consider moving to a row-based API instead of a bvtes-based API in the future.

The idea behind exposing the object store size (instead of fraction of dataset size) is:
1. it does not depend on what dataset is used, so it does not have to be changed based on which dataset is used
2. it's more directly related to what the user wants (e.g., prevent spilling vs. prioritize caching)
3. it's more compatible with autoscaling
The end state would be to set this to a good default like 50-80% and most users will never have to change it.

**Why we were initially converting to numpy?**
This was introduced in #5226. Before that PR all inputs were converted to a python list which led to an [enormous time for putting the value into ray storage](https://github.com/modin-project/modin/issues/5225#issue-1452326299). It was then suggested that python lists are generally much slower compared with NumPy arrays when it comes to serializing so it was proposed that we should always convert to NumPy. 

However, now making a closer look into this it appeared that `list()` serialization is actually quite fast when the list consists of python built-in types (int, float, str, list(2d list), ...) and the observed behavior of slow serialization only appears when a list suddenly consists of non-native types (np.int64, np.float, ...):
```python
'bad' list dtype: <class 'numpy.int64'>
'good' list dtype: <class 'int'>
Time for putting a numpy array: 0.05478150397539139
Time for putting a bad python list: 4.355332792038098
Time for putting a good python list: 0.026673782034777105
```
<details><summary>Reproducer</summary>

```python
import ray
import numpy as np
from timeit import default_timer as timer

ray.init()

numpy_array = np.random.randint(0, 10, size=1_000_000)
python_list_bad = list(numpy_array)
python_list_good = numpy_array.tolist()

print(f"'bad' list dtype: {type(python_list_bad[0])}")
print(f"'good' list dtype: {type(python_list_good[0])}")

t1 = timer()
ray.put(numpy_array)
print(f"Time for putting a numpy array: {timer() - t1}") # 0.05s

t1 = timer()
ray.put(python_list_bad)
print(f"Time for putting a bad python list: {timer() - t1}") # 4.35s

t1 = timer()
ray.put(python_list_good)
print(f"Time for putting a good python list: {timer() - t1}") # 0.02s
```

</details>

And it's now clear that the 'bad' case is exactly what was happening before #5226. Previously the values were deliberately converted to a list like `list(value)` and then it accidentally generated a 'bad' case where a list consists of non-native types:
```python
>>> ls = list(np.array([1, 2]))
>>> type(ls[0])
<class 'numpy.int32'>
```
In a real-world scenario, it's unlikely (I hope) that we would meet a list with numpy ints in it, thus I believe that we can safely remove all the `value` preprocessing and pass a raw value to ray's object store without losing anything in terms of performance.

There was a similar discussion on the [vectorize newton PR in SciPy](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.newton.html), and they opted to display a `RuntimeWarning`, unless the optional arg `disp=True` in which case raise [`RuntimeError`](../../../scipy/scipy/blob/master/scipy/optimize/zeros.py#L361). However, the use of the keyword `disp` was considered awkward, and only maintained  for backwards compatibility. The [statsmodels author's opinion was a return flag](../../../scipy/scipy/pull/9568#issuecomment-444168546), similar to Cliff's NaNs idea. Someone else thought it should fall back to another algorithm. Etc. Lot's of competing opinions, no right answer.

My personal opinion is to favor exceptions. I think in MATLAB and other languages we learn to fear exceptions, but Python tends to embrace them. I think the argument for raising an exception is that it informs the user and let's them quickly decide how to handle it. It also avoids a lot of type-checking and null-pointer testing which isn't necessary in a memory-managed language. If I were running an example with 2000 points, I would want to know right away that it wasn't working, so I could fix it and move on. The reason for this is because MXNet, unlike the other frameworks, has the ability to rescale gradients in the optimizer (via `rescale_grad`), usually with the scaling fused into the weight update kernel. In the past, this was a nice performance optimization because Horovod required calling a separate kernel for scaling gradients (or even worse, called a kernel per individual gradient for scaling). By using MXNet's gradient rescaling functionality, we were able to cut down on the number of kernels launched and improve performance. Since I fused scaling into the buffer packing, this has been of less benefit; however, I maintained the older path for platforms (e.g. ROCM) that didn't have the CUDA kernels I wrote to do the scaling/packing and could still benefit from the gradient scaling in the optimizer. Perhaps this is no longer needed since #3486, but it is hard to say without any CI testing of that platform.

Shouldn't we defer this until the operator has started being dispatched to?

As is, this will currently cause all `MapOperators` using actors to create their actor pools before the operator has been dispatched any inputs, i.e. before it's ready to execute, which could lead to a lot of wasted resources and maybe even a liveness issue (although the latter shouldn't happen due to our base resource accounting). I was thinking that it would be better for an operator to defer creating its actor pool until it has been dispatched its first input or is at least eligible for dispatch.

```python
# when we call 'df.groupby("a").fillna(0)' the keyword binding would resolve into something like:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: aggregate() got multiple values for argument 'func'
# instead we want the 'func' to be passed as a positional arg as well, so the fillna's arg
# would come after the func
>>> pandas.core.groupby.DataFrameGroupBy.aggregate(df.groupby("a"), "fillna", 0)
   b  c
0  3  a
1  2  b
2  5  c
```

One thing to think about is that prefixes are added differently during training and inference.

For training, users can add prefix tokens directly to the data because there's no chunking (sequences over max length are filtered out).  In this case, prefix tokens will be counted when computing length ratios.

During inference, the model treats each chunk as a separate sequence.  Including prefix tokens when computing the length of each chunk would match the training conditions.

```python
mask[0, 15:18, 20:32] = True

mask[1, 22:33, 22:33] = True

masks = mask.clone()

n, h, w = masks.shape

selem_size = dilation * 2 + 1
selem = torch.ones((1, 1, selem_size, selem_size), device=masks.device)

contours = masks.byte() - eroded_masks

fig, ax = plt.subplots(n, 3, figsize=(10, 10))

    ax[i, 2].imshow(contours[i], cmap='gray')

plt.show()
```
